Description: The Self-Controlled; rejected marriage, wine (using water for Eucharist), and meat; viewed reproduction as perpetuating the fallen world.
An apocalyptic movement claiming new revelation through the Paraclete; featured female leadership, ecstatic prophecy, and strict moral rigorism; condemned by bishops for bypassing clerical authority.
Churches that celebrated Pascha (Easter) on 14 Nisan (Jewish Passover) regardless of the day of the week, claiming authority from John the Apostle; clashed with Rome's Sunday observance.
Major_Proponents: Polycrates of Ephesus, Melito of Sardis

Description: A reactionary group that rejected the Gospel of John and the Apocalypse (Revelation) to counter Montanism and Gnostic Logos theology; denied the Logos doctrine.
Taught that Jesus was a mere man (virtuous) tested by God, upon whom the Spirit descended at baptism; denied Jesus was God by nature (Adoptionism).
Major_Proponents: Theodotus the Tanner, Theodotus the Banker

Description: Believed Father, Son, and Spirit were three modes or masks of one single person (Monad), not distinct persons; accused of Patripassianism (the Father suffered on the cross).
A Church of the Pure formed after the Decian persecution; refused to readmit the lapsi (those who sacrificed to idols) to communion; theologically orthodox but disciplinary hardliners.
Followers of Paul of Samosata; taught Jesus was a man who became divine through will and union with God's wisdom/Logos (not a distinct person but an attribute); condemned at Antioch (268).
A distinct world religion rivaling Christianity; Mani claimed to be the Apostle of Jesus Christ; synthesized Christianity, Zoroastrianism, and Buddhism into a rigorous dualist cosmology.
A rigorist schism parallel to Donatism; formed over the treatment of lapsed Christians during the Diocletian persecution; allied with Arians against the Bishop of Alexandria.
Major North African church rejecting traditors (clergy who handed over books during persecution); taught that sacraments are invalid if performed by unworthy priests; distinct Punic social identity.
The central controversy of the 4th century; taught that the Son was a created being (there was when he was not) and not co-eternal or consubstantial with the Father.
A pejorative name (Filthy Ones) for a late Gnostic group described by Epiphanius; allegedly practiced sacramental consumption of bodily fluids and extreme antinomianism.
A schismatic group that read image of God literally (Anthropomorphism), believing God has a human form; also quartodeciman in practice and morally rigorist.
The Praying Ones; rejected sacraments and hierarchy, believing only ceaseless prayer could expel the indwelling demon present in all humans; precursors to later mysticism.

The state-sanctioned church following the Council of Nicaea; established the Nicene Creed and the pentarchy of patriarchates (Rome, Constantinople, Alexandria, Antioch, Jerusalem) before later schisms.
Major_Proponents: Athanasius, Basil the Great, Gregory of Nazianzus

Description: The largest Eastern Catholic church; formed by the Union of Brest where Orthodox bishops in Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth accepted papal primacy while retaining Byzantine rite and married clergy.

Major_Proponents: Cyril VI Tanas, Maximos IV Sayegh

Description: The foundational order of Western Monasticism; independent abbeys living under the Rule of St. Benedict (Ora et Labora); historically preserved literacy and agriculture in Europe.
The most rigorist contemplative order; hermits living in community with near-total silence, solitude, and fasting; Never reformed because never deformed.
A reform of Benedictinism emphasizing manual labor, poverty, and seclusion; Trappists (17th C. reform) are famous for strict observance and beer/cheese production.
Mendicant order focused on radical poverty, preaching, and identification with the poor; split into various branches (Observants, Capuchins, Conventuals) over the strictness of poverty.
Mendicant order dedicated to preaching and combating heresy through intellectual rigor; dominated scholastic theology and the Inquisition; distinct democratic governance.
The Shock Troops of the Counter-Reformation; known for special vow of obedience to the Pope, missionary adaptability, education, and suppression of Protestantism.
A major medieval heresy flourishing in Catholic territories; dualists who rejected the material world (and Catholic sacraments) as evil; had a hierarchy of Perfects and Believers.
Monastic warrior order protecting pilgrims; became early bankers of Europe; suppressed by Pope Clement V and King Philip IV of France on charges of heresy and sodomy.
Major_Proponents: Hugues de Payens, Jacques de Molay

Description: A theological movement emphasizing original sin, human depravity, and irresistible grace (similar to Calvinism); condemned by Rome but deeply influential in French piety.

A movement within the Catholic Church emphasizing baptism in the Holy Spirit, tongues, and prophecy, similar to Pentecostalism but remaining doctrinally Catholic.

Description: Separatists who rejected Patriarch Nikon's liturgical reforms (e.g., three-finger sign of the cross); maintained pre-17th-century Russian rites; heavily persecuted historically.
A specific branch of Old Believers (Priested) who fled Russia to the Danube Delta; they maintain strict Old Rite traditions and distinct Slavic ethno-religious identity.
Various groups that separated from mainstream Eastern Orthodoxy after the adoption of the Revised Julian Calendar; they view the mainstream church as fallen into the heresy of Ecumenism.
The monastic-centered church of the British Isles; distinct calculation of Easter, tonsure, and penitential systems; eventually absorbed by the Roman Rite after the Synod of Whitby (664).
A dualist, adoptionist sect; rejected the Old Testament, sacraments, and hierarchy; highly militarized and fiercely iconoclastic; influenced later Balkan heresies.
A neo-Gnostic peasant movement; taught that the material world was created by Satan; rejected the church hierarchy, baptism, and the cross; influenced the Western Cathars.
The official state church during the Iconoclast periods; condemned the veneration of images as idolatry and persecuted Iconodules; theology emphasized the Eucharist as the only true image.
Spirit Wrestlers; a radical pacifist Russian sect rejecting liturgy, icons, scripture, and priesthood; believe God resides in the soul of the faithful; historically migrated to Canada.
Milk Drinkers (drank milk on fast days); rejected the institutional church, icons, and state authority; strictly biblical but interpret scripture spiritually/allegorically.
Russian peasants who adopted Old Testament practices (Saturday Sabbath, circumcision, dietary laws); some fully converted to Judaism, others remained Karaite/Christian hybrids.
A Soviet-backed modernist schism from the Russian Orthodox Church; attempted to reconcile Christianity with Communism, allowed married bishops, and removed monarchist symbols.
Communities within canonical Eastern/Oriental Orthodox churches that use restored Western (Gregorian/Tikhon) liturgies rather than the Byzantine Rite; theologically Orthodox but culturally Western.

The oldest surviving proto-Protestant group; began as a lay preaching movement emphasizing poverty and memorization of vernacular scripture; rejected indulgences, purgatory, and oaths; later merged with the Reformed tradition.

Followers of Wycliffe who anticipated the English Reformation; advocated for a vernacular English Bible, rejected transubstantiation (remnance), and critiqued clerical wealth and the papacy as Antichrist.
Major_Proponents: John Wycliffe, Nicholas of Hereford, John Oldcastle

Description: A radical anti-clerical movement; rejected infant baptism, the construction of church buildings (faith is in the person, not the stone), the veneration of crosses (which they burned), and prayers for the dead.

Advocated for strict apostolic poverty and the surrender of the Church's temporal power/property to the state; briefly established a Roman commune defying the Pope; precursors to separation of church and state.

The moderate wing of the Hussite movement; demanded communion in both kinds (bread and cup) for the laity (the chalice became their symbol); maintained traditional liturgy but rejected total papal control.

The militant, apocalyptic wing of the Hussites; rejected everything not explicitly found in Scripture (icons, purgatory, saints, fasts); established a communal society awaiting the return of Christ; crushed at the Battle of Lipany.
A pacifist separation from the Utraquists; emphasized the Sermon on the Mount, non-violence, and simple living; the direct ancestors of the modern Moravian Church (Herrnhuters).

Description: A fringe group expelled by Taborites; attempted to return to an Edenic state of innocence, often practicing nudity and rejecting marriage/social norms; eventually annihilated by Ziska.

Rejected the institutional church's wealth; roamed as itinerant preachers mimicking the apostles; eventually radicalized into an armed insurgency under Dolcino, predicting the destruction of the Whore of Babylon (the Church).

A pietist community of laypeople living in common without vows; emphasized inner spirituality, education, and copying scriptures; deeply influenced Luther and Erasmus (the Reform before the Reformation).

A loose network of mystics (lay and clergy) seeking direct union with God amidst the Babylonian Captivity of the Papacy; prioritized inner experience over external sacraments; produced the Theologia Germanica.
Major_Proponents: Johannes Tauler, Henry Suso, Rulman Merswin

Description: The Shorn Ones; early Russian dissenters who rejected the hierarchy due to simony (selling offices); rejected confession to priests, claiming only God forgives.

An enigmatic group in Russia that denied the Trinity and divinity of Christ, rejected icons/monasticism, and adhered to Old Testament laws; historically termed Judaizers but functionally early Unitarian/Iconoclasts.

Denomination_Name: Free Spirit (Brethren of the Free Spirit)
Description: A diffuse heresy teaching that the perfected soul is one with God and thus above moral law and church sacraments; while more Gnostic, their anti-clericalism and rejection of church authority parallels Protestant radicalism.

The Weepers; followers of the friar Savonarola who established a theocratic republic in Florence; burned vanities (art/cosmetics); though theologically Catholic, their denunciation of papal corruption paved the way for the Reformation.

The original Protestant tradition; emphasizes Sola Fide, the Real Presence (Sacramental Union), and the distinction between Law and Gospel; historically organized as state churches in Germany and Scandinavia.
Major_Proponents: Martin Luther, Philip Melanchthon, Johannes Bugenhagen

Description: A small but distinct group maintaining the Plain tradition; they hold to unprogrammed worship and traditional Quaker speech/dress, resisting both liberalism and evangelicalism.
A branch of Quakerism that adopted programmed worship (pastors/music) and evangelical theology; emphasizes the authority of Scripture and missions over silent waiting.
Founded to focus on global missions; teaches the Fourfold Gospel (Jesus as Savior, Sanctifier, Healer, and Coming King); non-Pentecostal but emphasizes divine healing.
Formed by Scandinavian immigrants; distinct for its no-creed-but-the-Bible stance and radical congregational autonomy; a central pillar of American Evangelicalism.
Emerged from Swedish Lutheran Mission Friends; emphasizes the necessity of the new birth and a mixture of Lutheran heritage with free-church congregationalism.
Congregations that left mainline denominations during the Modernist-Fundamentalist controversy; typically adhere to Dispensationalism and strict Biblical inerrancy.
A church-growth strategy (Willow Creek/Saddleback) that adapts the church environment to be more accessible to non-believers, often removing traditional religious symbols.
A movement found within mainline churches (UCC, TEC) and independent circles; emphasizes social justice, environmentalism, and a non-literal/metaphorical interpretation of dogma.
Major_Proponents: Marcus Borg, John Shelby Spong, Rachel Held Evans
